definitions: A widebody aircraft whose aft fuselage exhibits significantly more curvature than its fore fuselage; esp. one parked tail-toward the camera at golden hour.
colloq. Any commercial jet you'd describe as "built."
example: That 747 is straight-up a thiccc Boeing. The empennage on her? Architectural.
etymology: From thiccc (internet vernacular, c. 2015, "voluptuous; full-bodied," with an extra c for emphasis) + Boeing Company (Seattle-based aircraft manufacturer, est. 1916). First attested on Thiccctionary.com, May 2026.
caption: Plate I. ‚ÄĒ A specimen of considerable rear-end displacement.

definitions: A pear-shaped fruit (Persea americana) of disproportionate hip-to-waist ratio, often photographed sliced for visual emphasis.
Any specimen exceeding 400g and exhibiting what botanists term 'a generous undercarriage'.
example: Cut it in half ‚ÄĒ this avocado is doing serious work.
etymology: From Spanish aguacate, from Nahuatl ńĀhuacatl, originally meaning "testicle" ‚ÄĒ which, frankly, tracks.
caption: Plate II. ‚ÄĒ Note the pronounced lower bulb.

definitions: A pickup truck of such posterior magnitude that it requires dual rear wheels per side to remain in physical contact with the road.
slang. A vehicle that, were it sentient, would be described as 'thiccc with three Cs.'
example: Pulled into the diner lot in his F-450 ‚ÄĒ back end wider than the booth he sat in.
etymology: From Henry Ford (industrialist) + the model code for the heaviest-duty pickup in the lineup. The numerical suffix correlates positively with girth.
caption: Plate III. ‚ÄĒ Dually configuration, viewed astern.

definitions: A cultivar of Solanum lycopersicum whose girth defies its stem; structurally suspect, aesthetically blessed.
Any tomato that, if it were furniture, would require two people to move.
example: Three pounds. One tomato. They had to use two hands at the farmer's market.
etymology: From heritage (denoting ancestral cultivars) + tomato. The word "heritage" here implies that the tomato has a sense of history, gravitas, and lower-body presence.
caption: Plate IV. ‚ÄĒ A Brandywine of unusual proportions.

definitions: An industrial vehicle of contemplative rotundity, characterized by a single, slowly-rotating, drum-shaped midsection.
The platonic ideal of thicccness: all body, no apologies.
example: She stopped traffic. Eight cubic yards of slow-spinning confidence.
etymology: From concrete (Latin concretus, "grown together") + mixer. The drum's rotation is, for our purposes, decorative.
caption: Plate V. ‚ÄĒ At rest. Engine idle. Drum rotating.

definitions: A piece of upholstered seating, c. 1955‚Äď1970, distinguished by an aggressively curved backside and tapered wooden legs that seem to be holding up too much.
Any chair you'd describe as 'a whole mood' but also 'a whole structural concern.'
example: That Eames knock-off is a thiccc piece of furniture. The cushion alone is a continent.
etymology: From mid-century (denoting an era of design optimism and tapered furniture legs) + armchair. The era's commitment to organic curves was, in retrospect, very on-brand.
caption: Plate VI. ‚ÄĒ Walnut frame. Wool upholstery. Notable rear cushioning.
